items.answer:
To make Solar work for you home or business you need Solar Panels, an Inverter and (optional) Battery Storage.

Here's how all of that works together:

☀️ Solar Panels

  • Capture sunlight and generate DC electricity (direct current)

  • Raw electricity that can't be used directly in your home as-is

  • Output varies with light levels throughout the day

⚡ Inverter

  • The "brain" of the system

  • Converts DC electricity from the panels into AC electricity (alternating current) — which is what your home appliances run on

  • Also manages the flow of electricity between panels, battery, home, and grid

  • Modern hybrid inverters handle everything in one unit

🔋 Battery

  • Stores excess electricity generated during the day that your home isn't using in that moment

  • At night (or on cloudy days) when panels aren't producing enough, the battery discharges to power your home

  • Without a battery, unused daytime electricity goes straight to the grid
items.question: Solar: How does it all work?

items.answer: Most homes are good candidates if they have a south-, east-, or west-facing roof with minimal shading, in good structural condition. A site assessment will confirm your home's solar potential.
items.question: Is my home a good candidate for solar?

items.answer: Most panels are warrantied for 25–30 years and often perform well beyond that. Degradation is typically less than 0.5% per year.

items.answer: We're based in Dublin but we cover all of Ireland — from Dublin to Cork, Galway, Limerick, and everywhere in between. If you're not sure, just give us a shout and we'll let you know!

items.answer: We're normally around 8am – 5pm, but we actually offer a 24-hour service if you need us. We're always just a phone call away.

items.answer: Costs vary based on system size, roof complexity, and equipment. Most residential systems range from €8,000–€18,000 before incentives and SEAI grants.
items.question: How much does a solar installation cost?

items.answer: Most of our customers save around €2,000 a year on their energy bills — some even more! With a well-designed system, you could cut your bills by up to 70%. Not bad, right?
items.question: How much will I actually save?

items.answer: The SEAI offers grants of up to €2,100 for solar PV panels and €600 for battery storage. We handle all the grant paperwork for you, making the process hassle-free.
items.question: What SEAI grants are available?

items.answer: The SEAI (Sustainable Energy Authority of Ireland) offers grants for solar PV, battery storage, and heat pumps. Many homeowners can access up to €2,100 for solar panels alone. We guide you through all available incentives to maximise your savings.
items.question: What incentives or tax credits are available?

items.answer: Options typically include cash purchase, solar loans, and flexible payment plans. Each has different ownership and savings implications — we'll help you find the best fit for your budget.
items.question: What financing options do you offer?

items.answer: Most homeowners see a payback period of 5–8 years, depending on local electricity rates, system size, and financing. After that, it's essentially free electricity.
items.question: How long until I break even?

items.answer: It typically involves a site assessment, system design, permitting, installation (usually 1–2 days), utility interconnection, and final inspection — often 4–8 weeks start to finish.
items.question: What does the installation process look like?

items.answer: Most home installs are done in just 1-2 days. We turn up on time, keep everything spotless, and get you generating your own power before you know it.
items.question: How long does it take to install?

items.answer: No. Professional installers use flashing and mounting hardware designed to maintain your roof's integrity. We offer a roof penetration warranty for complete peace of mind.
items.question: Will installation damage my roof?

items.answer: You don't need to be present the whole time, but someone should be available at the start and end of the installation day.
items.question: Do I need to be home during installation?

items.answer: Our team always arrive with ID badges, in clean uniform, and ready to go. If you're ever unsure, just call the office and we'll confirm who's on the way.
items.question: How will I know who's turning up at my house?

items.answer: Yes! Solar panels work with daylight, not just direct sunshine. Ireland gets plenty of diffused light even on overcast days. While output is lower than on a sunny day, modern panels are highly efficient and still generate meaningful electricity year-round.
items.question: Does solar work in cloudy weather?

items.answer: Panels still generate some electricity on cloudy days. At night, you draw from the grid or a battery storage system. With a well-sized battery, you can use your stored solar energy through the evening.
items.question: What happens on cloudy days or at night?

items.answer: Very little. Occasional cleaning and an annual inspection are typically all that's needed. Most issues are caught through remote monitoring, so problems are rare and quickly resolved.
items.question: Do solar panels require maintenance?

items.answer: Net metering (or micro-generation export) lets you send excess solar energy back to the grid in exchange for bill credits, effectively using the grid as a 'battery.' In Ireland, the Clean Export Guarantee allows you to earn for every kWh you export.
items.question: What is net metering?

items.answer:
Good question

You've taken the step of installing solar panels — or you're seriously thinking about it. Now comes the next question almost every homeowner asks: do I need a battery too? The honest answer is: it depends. But for many Irish households, the answer is a resounding yes. Here's why.

What Does a Solar Battery Actually Do?

Solar panels generate electricity during daylight hours — typically between 9am and 4pm. But most Irish households use the most electricity in the evenings, when panels produce little or nothing. Without a battery, any excess electricity your panels generate during the day gets exported to the grid. With a battery, that surplus energy is stored and ready to use when you need it most.

When a Battery Makes a Lot of Sense

A battery is likely a great investment for you if:

•       You're out of the house during the day — if nobody is home while the panels are generating, most of that energy would otherwise go straight to the grid for a low return.

•       You use a lot of electricity in the evenings — cooking, charging an EV, running appliances after work.

•       You want to reduce your reliance on the grid — and protect yourself from rising electricity prices.

•       You have a larger solar system — more panels means more surplus to store.

•       You're interested in backup power — some batteries can keep your home running during a grid outage.

 

When a Battery is Less Essential

A battery may be less of a priority if someone is at home throughout the day using electricity as it's generated, or if you're working with a tighter budget. In that case, solar panels alone still deliver strong returns and you can always add a battery later.
items.question: Do I need a battery?

items.answer: Batteries are ideal if you want backup power during outages, have time-of-use utility rates, or want to maximise energy independence. They add cost but significantly increase resilience and self-consumption of your solar energy.
items.question: Should I add battery storage?

items.answer: Without a battery, grid-tied systems shut off for safety. With a battery backup, you can keep essential circuits running — lights, fridge, internet, and more — until the grid comes back online.
items.question: What happens during a power outage?

items.answer: Absolutely! We are SEAI Approved Installers with over 20 years of experience. We hold all the necessary certifications and qualifications. You're in very safe hands.
